metadata.title: A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Newspapers Coverage of Hong Kong Protests Between 1998 and 2020

abstract: This article investigates how the SCMP, the China Daily-and western-based newspapers cover protests in Hong Kong in an effort to identify changes in journalistic practices between 1998 and 2020. It combines natural language processing (NLP) with a qualitative investigation of a novel corpus of newspaper articles spanning 22 years. It enlists topic modeling to contrast the treatment of protests in Hong Kong diachronically and across news sources. Through comparison of lexical frequency and lexical usage it showcases preferences and discrepancies in the use of protest-relevant keywords in the newspapers' articles. Embedding neighborhood comparisons strengthens our understanding of how words are used differently between the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers, and also how the context of protest-related keywords may differ across news sources over time. Finally, computational sentiment analysis measures the tone and connotations of articles. The article fills a gap in the literature on Hong Kong media and its methodology broadens the application of NLP techniques to the social sciences.

paragraphs: Protest participation has long been an undercurrent in Hong Kong's political culture (Lam 2004), even though large-scale demonstrations have become more prominent in the posthandover era. On July 1, 1997, Beijing regained sovereignty over Hong Kong, and on July 2, 1997, thousands of people marched the streets of Hong Kong, in the rain, demanding democracy. Since then, the anniversary of the handover has been marked by both official celebrations and street marches, with varied citizens' turnout depending on the social and political issues gripping Hong Kong. In 2003, for instance, the July 1 march saw the participation of an estimated 500,000 citizens who protested the proposed National Security Bill,foot_0 and resulted in the proposed law to be dropped from the Legislative Council's agenda. (Lawrence & Martin, 2020). As protests and rallies became more frequent, with participation ranging anywhere from tens of thousands to millions (Chan & Lee, 2007), they "also became normalized and started to lose their power" (Chan and Lee, 2011: 118-120) which, in turn, created incentives for protests' escalation and radicalization (Cheng, 2014).

The 2006 Star and Queen protest against the demolition of the Edinburgh Place Ferry Pier and the Queen's Pier lasted for ninety-seven days and turned the piers into symbols of collective identity in Hong Kong. In 2012, the Scholarism movement 2 brought together secondary school students and teachers to oppose lawmakers' attempt to impose a Moral and National Education curriculum (M&NEC). A parade of 90,000 people was held at the end of July. On 1 September protesters held a carnival-demonstration, following which the government suspended the M&NEC on September 8, and abandoned the proposal completely on September 11, following Hong Kong Federation of Students' half-day class boycott (Wang, 2017). It was, however, the 2014 Occupy Central protests that caught the attention of the press both in Hong Kong and abroad. The Chinese government had promised that the 2017 election of the chief executive would be by universal suffrage. However, on June 10, 2014, to make sure that no subversive politician would be elected as chief executive, Beijing issued a white paper on the principle of one country, two systems, stating that "the high degree of autonomy of the Hong Kong-SAR is (…) subject to central leadership's authorization." 3 Then, on August 31, Beijing announced a new electoral framework for Hong Kong, in which candidates would need to obtain support from more than half of the nomination committee's 1,200 members to stand in the election. Hongkongers responded to this announcement with Occupy Central, a disobedience campaign intended to force the Chinese government to grant universal suffrage to Hong Kong, which turned into the Umbrella Movement, and the intermittent occupation of downtown Hong Kong from September 28th to December 15th of 2014.

Protest radicalization continued in 2016, a year punctuated by multiple episodes of civic unrest to counter what Hongkongers perceived as Beijing's growing unease with calls for democracy and autonomy. Some of most heated protests erupted over a new election procedure requiring candidates running for the LegCo's elections to sign a form acknowledging Hong Kong as an inseparable part of China. 4 It was, however, the 2019-2020 protests against the amendment to the Fugitive Offenders and Mutual Legal Assistance in Criminal Matters Legislation Bill (ELAB) 5 that set new highs for protests' violence and radicalization. Opponents to ELAB argued that the proposed amendment would weaken the one country, two systems provisions for Hong Kong's judiciary to remain independent and "effectively remove the legal firewall between the city and the mainland" (Ibrahim and Lam, 2020: 54). Early demonstrations to voice dissent morphed into an increasingly forceful anti-government, anti-Beijing movement. Hong Kong citizens' militancy captured domestic and international media attention. 6 The news value of the protests increased as they became the largest in Hong Kong's history-and the largest to date on Chinese soil-and their objectives shifted from protesting the amendment to the extradition bill to protecting democracy.

Given the relationship between the news and protests, this article investigates how Hong Kong-and western-based newspapers cover protests in an effort to identify changes in journalistic practices between 1998 and 2020. To meet the challenges inherent in operationalizing our research we used a mixed-method approach that combines natural language processing (NLP) with a qualitative investigation of patterns of journalistic practice across newspapers. (Atteveldt and Peng, 2018;Hilbert et al., 2019;Baden et al., 2021;and Brady, 2019). As language is at the heart of our research, NLP emerges as especially important for its ability "to analyze signals ranging from simple lexical clues to word clusters to choices of syntactic structure" (Boydstun et al., 2013: 2) as well as its speed, scale, granularity, and reliability when analyzing text. Our analysis uses a novel corpus of newspaper articles spanning 22 years, and enlists several NLP techniques to investigate changes in press freedom in Hong Kong. Topic modeling contrasts the treatment of protests in Hong Kong diachronically and across news sources. Comparison of lexical frequency and lexical usage reveals preferences and discrepancies in the use of protest-relevant keywords in the newspapers' articles. Computational sentiment analysis measures the tone and connotations of articles. Embedding neighborhood comparisons strengthens our understanding of how words are used differently between western-and Hong Kong-based newspapers, and also how the context of protest-related keywords may differ across news sources over time.

In its choice of methodology, the article broadens the application of NLP techniques to the social sciences "without diluting conceptual standards or privileging the study of phenomena that are easily measurable" (Baden et al., 2021: 2). In doing so, it helps narrow the gap between "social scientific, validity-focused methodological debates, and the prevalent mode of technologically driven computational text analysis methods development" (Baden et al., 2021: 13). In its findings, the article fills a gap in the literature on Hong Kong media (Lee, 2014;Tsfati and Walter, 2019).

The biggest section of Hong Kong, the New Territories, was scheduled to revert to mainland rule in 1997, and real-estate investors feared they would lose everything in the reversion. paragraphs: ) while remaining aware that Chinese, North American and British newspapers sit at opposite ends of the spectrum in terms of ownership and state control. Hong Kong has a long legacy of an aggressive media in both Cantonese and English, with newspapers being widely read, especially for their sharp critiques of government and police failures, and the forces of de-colonization shaping the development of the English-language news media in Hong Kong over the past two decades. When in the mid-1980s Hong Kong began the transition to Chinese sovereignty, the position of influence of local English-language press began to erode while Chinese-language newspapers begun expanding their readership and international English-language media conglomerates, mostly US-owned, boosting their presence. English-language news media in Hong Kong is very influential, and market mechanisms do not explain why they wield such influence. As for plurality and freedom of the media, the Hong Kong media landscape fits with the findings of Djankov et al. (2003), which show that almost universally the largest media firms are owned by the government or by private families-with government ownership being more pervasive in broadcasting than in the printed media-as well as those of Gehlbach and Sonin (2014). In contrast with the realities of the Hong Kong media, the US and the UK media have been characterized by the centrality of large-scale industries since the development of the penny press in the 1830s (Thompson, 2004). For several decades in the mid-twentieth century, an equilibrium existed in the media system, with stable markets that made the media companies highly profitable and very influential as social institutions. Newspapers invested in newsrooms, and the journalist profession grew in autonomy and influence. Journalism was characterized by a low level of political parallelism, with objectivity dominating journalistic ethics. Over time, however, economic, technological, and political change began disrupting those very elements that guaranteed the stability and fairness of the press system in the late twentieth century. Stable boundaries that once separated markets have been disrupted by digital convergence and deregulation. Changing business models impacted newsrooms' sizes, and the Internet has accelerated the fragmentation of broadcast industries. Newspapers' political parallelism has increased with little concern about reporting-related pitfalls, including declining public trust.

Newspapers' ideological orientation plays a fundamental role in how events are portrayed and received domestically and internationally. To ensure plurality of views, the article corpus comes from six western-based, English language newspapers: The New York Times (NYT), The Wall Street Journal (WSJ), The Washington Post (WaPo), The Financial Times (FT), The Guardian, and The Times; and the China Daily and the South China Morning Post (SCMP), two Hong Kong-based, English language newspapers which are ideologically apart. The China Daily, in fact, is the largest English-language daily in China, according to its mission statement, "an authoritative provider of information, analysis, comment and entertainment for global readers with a special focus on China."foot_2 Its views are seen as indicative of the policy positions of the Chinese government, though is considered more liberal than the other Chinese state newspapers that circulate in Hong Kong (Hartig 2017 and2019). Founded in 1903 the manifest goal of supporting the reform movement in China, the SCMP has long been the broadsheet of the city's elites, and it is arguably the city's most important title internationally, a position gained from being a sizeable English-language outlet in a strategically important city and a tradition of credible reporting, especially on issues that might be considered sensitive in mainland China.

Current NLP limitations of comparison across languages (Bender, 2009;Baden et al., 2021) ultimately resulted in the decision to include only Hong Kong-and western-based English language newspapers (Earl et al., 2004;Lee, 2014;Du et al., 2018;Baden et al., 2021). While multilingual NLP approaches are being proposed, they are typically proven only for languages related to English (Mimno et al., 2011;Reber, 2019;Chan et al., 2020). In fact, Bender (2009) and Pires et al. (2019) discuss the limitations generally, and Baden et al. (2021) discuss specific issues in multilingual NLP for the social sciences. Further, recent WMT shared task (Barrault et al., 2019) shows why translation as a preprocessing step is a suboptimal option (Field et al., 2018) as current translation systems struggle to maintain coherence across long document contexts. The fact that English is one of the two official languages of Hong Kong (Basic Law, Article 9), with 52% of the population speaking it, and that the SCMP8 and China Daily are the printed English language newspapers with the largest readership in Hong Kong further validates our choice. Finally, our choice of relying on selected Hong Kong English language newspapers is consistent with the work of Bhatia (2015), Yu (2015); Wong and Liu (2018); Du et al. (2018) and Lee (2014), who also use English language news sources to investigate selected episodes of protests in Hong Kong.

We use news articles focusing on eight non-randomly selected episodes of civic unrest in Hong Kong. We focus on (i) the 1998-2002 July anniversary marches; (ii) the 2003 protests against the national security reform; (iii) the 2004-2019 July 1 protests; (iv) the 2006 Star and Queen protests; v) the 2012 protests against Moral and National Education; (vi) the 2014 Occupy Central protests; (vii) the 2016 Riots; and (viii) the 2019-2020 anti-ELAB protests. Taken together, these protests represent a sustained citizens' effort to safeguard Hong Kong from the loss of civil and political liberties (Sparks, 2015;Chan 2015).

The articles were collected through keyword-based searches in ProQuest Newspapers for western English language newspapers, and Newsbank Access World News Research Collection for Hong Kong English language newspapers. We searched for the keywords "Hong Kong" + "protests", "Hong Kong" + "rallies", "Hong Kong" + "marches", and "Hong Kong" + "riots". We used the East Coast edition for the NYT and WSJ, the UK edition for the FT, The Guardian, and The Times, and Hong Kong edition for China Daily. To be eligible for collection, articles had to be at least 300 words long, and focus on the protests. A one-by-one, manual screening process eliminated irrelevant items such as eventual duplicates within each publication, readers' letters, and articles that included any of the chosen keywords but whose content was not about the protests. Following the manual screening, we retained 793 articles from western-based newspapers and 3729 from Hong Kong-based newspapers, with a mean length of 783 tokens. A total 4522 articles make up the Hong Kong Protest News Dataset, the original curated collection that informs our research.

The Hong Kong Protest News Dataset has a 22-year time horizon, spanning from January 1, 1998, to June 30, 2020, to capture changes in how Hong Kong-and westernbased newspapers covered protests in Hong Kong, and to test the meaning and robustness of eventual changes of newspaper treatment of the protests over time. The extended time horizon together with the size of our sample departs from current research practice, which favors the treatment of smaller samples of articles, generally in the hundreds, focuses on a particular episode of protest, or compares no more than two episodes protests at different points in time. Table 1 contrasts the scope and scale of our dataset with previous corpora focused on protests in Hong Kong, the largest of which has 40% as many articles spanning half as many years.

To ensure plurality of views, the article corpus comes from six western-based, English language newspapers: The New York Times (NYT), The Wall Street Journal (WSJ), The Washington Post (WaPo), The Financial Times (FT), The Guardian, and The Times; and the China Daily and the South China Morning Post (SCMP), two Hong Kong-based, English language newspapers which are ideologically apart. The China Daily, in fact, is the largest English-language daily in China, according to its mission statement, "an authoritative provider of information, analysis, comment and entertainment for global readers with a special focus on China."foot_2 Its views are seen as indicative of the policy positions of the Chinese government, though is considered more liberal than the other Chinese state newspapers that circulate in Hong Kong (Hartig 2017 and2019). Founded in 1903 the manifest goal of supporting the reform movement in China, the SCMP has long been the broadsheet of the city's elites, and it is arguably the city's most important title internationally, a position gained from being a sizeable English-language outlet in a strategically important city and a tradition of credible reporting, especially on issues that might be considered sensitive in mainland China.

Current NLP limitations of comparison across languages (Bender, 2009;Baden et al., 2021) ultimately resulted in the decision to include only Hong Kong-and western-based English language newspapers (Earl et al., 2004;Lee, 2014;Du et al., 2018;Baden et al., 2021). While multilingual NLP approaches are being proposed, they are typically proven only for languages related to English (Mimno et al., 2011;Reber, 2019;Chan et al., 2020). In fact, Bender (2009) and Pires et al. (2019) discuss the limitations generally, and Baden et al. (2021) discuss specific issues in multilingual NLP for the social sciences. Further, recent WMT shared task (Barrault et al., 2019) shows why translation as a preprocessing step is a suboptimal option (Field et al., 2018) as current translation systems struggle to maintain coherence across long document contexts. The fact that English is one of the two official languages of Hong Kong (Basic Law, Article 9), with 52% of the population speaking it, and that the SCMP8 and China Daily are the printed English language newspapers with the largest readership in Hong Kong further validates our choice. Finally, our choice of relying on selected Hong Kong English language newspapers is consistent with the work of Bhatia (2015), Yu (2015); Wong and Liu (2018); Du et al. (2018) and Lee (2014), who also use English language news sources to investigate selected episodes of protests in Hong Kong.

We use news articles focusing on eight non-randomly selected episodes of civic unrest in Hong Kong. We focus on (i) the 1998-2002 July anniversary marches; (ii) the 2003 protests against the national security reform; (iii) the 2004-2019 July 1 protests; (iv) the 2006 Star and Queen protests; v) the 2012 protests against Moral and National Education; (vi) the 2014 Occupy Central protests; (vii) the 2016 Riots; and (viii) the 2019-2020 anti-ELAB protests. Taken together, these protests represent a sustained citizens' effort to safeguard Hong Kong from the loss of civil and political liberties (Sparks, 2015;Chan 2015).

The articles were collected through keyword-based searches in ProQuest Newspapers for western English language newspapers, and Newsbank Access World News Research Collection for Hong Kong English language newspapers. We searched for the keywords "Hong Kong" + "protests", "Hong Kong" + "rallies", "Hong Kong" + "marches", and "Hong Kong" + "riots". We used the East Coast edition for the NYT and WSJ, the UK edition for the FT, The Guardian, and The Times, and Hong Kong edition for China Daily. To be eligible for collection, articles had to be at least 300 words long, and focus on the protests. A one-by-one, manual screening process eliminated irrelevant items such as eventual duplicates within each publication, readers' letters, and articles that included any of the chosen keywords but whose content was not about the protests. Following the manual screening, we retained 793 articles from western-based newspapers and 3729 from Hong Kong-based newspapers, with a mean length of 783 tokens. A total 4522 articles make up the Hong Kong Protest News Dataset, the original curated collection that informs our research.

The Hong Kong Protest News Dataset has a 22-year time horizon, spanning from January 1, 1998, to June 30, 2020, to capture changes in how Hong Kong-and westernbased newspapers covered protests in Hong Kong, and to test the meaning and robustness of eventual changes of newspaper treatment of the protests over time. The extended time horizon together with the size of our sample departs from current research practice, which favors the treatment of smaller samples of articles, generally in the hundreds, focuses on a particular episode of protest, or compares no more than two episodes protests at different points in time. Table 1 contrasts the scope and scale of our dataset with previous corpora focused on protests in Hong Kong, the largest of which has 40% as many articles spanning half as many years.

sections.paper_id: Dore - 2023 - A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Newspapers Coverage of Hong Kong Protests Between 1998 and
paragraphs: To meet the challenges inherent in operationalizing our research we used a mixed-method approach that combines NLP techniques with a qualitative content investigation of patterns of journalistic practice across newspapers.

As language is at the heart of our research, NLP emerges as especially important for its ability "to analyze signals ranging from simple lexical clues to word clusters to choices of syntactic structure" (Boydstun et al., 2013: 2) as well as its speed, scale, granularity, and reliability when analyzing text. Our choice builds on existing application-based validation efforts, and the ongoing debate on the operational implications of computational procedures (Atteveldt & Peng, 2018;Hilbert et al., 2019;Brady, 2019;and Baden et al., 2021). We also benefit from the work of Wueest et al. (2013), who apply topic models and named entity recognition to protest event analysis, Hürriyetoğlu et al. (2019) who show that, computationally speaking, not all protests are alike as event extraction models for protests perform much worse on countries outside the training set, Lucy et al. (2020) on the use of word embedding similarity, topic models, and dependency parsing to generate clues toward differing portrayals of race and gender in US history textbooks; Field et al. (2018), who relate the content of Russian state-run news articles to the nation's economic performance, finding an agenda of distraction; and Bednarek et al. (2021) on word frequency and collocation for identifying news values. Also of relevance, Wijaya and Yeniterzi (2011) who show that relevant historical events lead to differences in word usage. Rudolph and Blei (2018) and Hofmann et al. (2021) discuss how word vectors may be fitted with the temporal or social context in mind, whereas Blei and Lafferty (2006;2007) track shifts in entire topics, rather than in the semantics of individual words.

Following Krippendorf ( 2004), we performed qualitative content analysis on a random sample of 30% of the article corpus to complement the findings emerging from the computational analysis. We performed decontextualization to familiarize ourselves with the data-through reading the text to get a sense of the content. Then, we selected the level of granularity relevant to the inquiry (Catanzaro, 1988;Graneheim & Lundman, 2004). In the case of our corpus, individual articles represent the meaning unit of our research. Through recontextualization, we checked whether the content of the articles is pertinent to the topic (in our case, the Hong Kong protests) which drives the research questions (Burnard, 1991). Finally, we performed categorization. Qualitative analysis of the articles' content was performed manually with the help of independent human annotators. While pre-identified categories may initially guide the study, others may emerge while performing the course of the analysis (Aditi 2015). As such, when the quantitative findings suggested additional categories (such as whether discuss the duty of police to maintain order), these were incorporated into the qualitative investigation. The results were cross-checked between four independent annotators and only analyses with high inter-annotator agreement were retained. The interplay of statistical techniques from NLP with qualitative content analysis results in a powerful dynamic where the predictive validity (i.e., expected correspondence between a measure and exogenous events uninvolved in the measurement process) and convergent validity (i.e., correlation with other measures of the same construct) (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013;Quinn et al., 2010) of the methods corroborate our findings.

sections.paper_id: Dore - 2023 - A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Newspapers Coverage of Hong Kong Protests Between 1998 and
paragraphs: A broad portfolio of approaches can help investigate the semantic relationships between topics present in documents (Lau et al. 2014). Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) (Deerwester et al. 1990) is highly intuitive but demanding in terms of pre-processing to obtain a significant representation from the textual input data. Probabilistic Latent Semantic Analysis (pLSA) (Hofmann, 1999), a variation of LSA, shows improved performances vis a vis LSA. Another variation of LSA, Non-Negative Matrix Factorization (Lee et al., 1999) furthers the analysis of shorter texts, such as tweets or titles, and produces more interpretable topics (Lee et al. 1999;Xu et al. 2003;Casalino et al., 2016). Then, the Pachinko Allocation Model (Li & McCallum, 2006) provides an alternative to Blei and Lafferty's work on dynamic topic modeling (2006). To contrast the treatment of protests in Hong Kong, both across news sources and over time, we use latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003), a probabilistic generative model that maintains distributions over the words within each topic and the topics with each article, representing each article in the traditional vector space model (Salton et al., 1975). This choice was informed by LDA working optimally with longer texts such as full articles, essays and books (Nguyen et al. 2015;McCarthy et al., 2021;Stede and Patz 2021;Ylä-Anttila et al. 2021), and the fact that LDA was to be performed on a single, manually curated dataset (i.e., The Hong Kong Protest News Dataset) whose construction provided insights valuable for the analysis of results emerging from the topic modeling.

We use MALLET (McCallum, 2002) to perform LDA (Blei et al., 2003), testing several numbers of topics k, evaluating their coherence score (Mimno et al., 2011), selecting the highest-coherence model, and hand-validating. Our topic model represents each article as a mixture of topics. More prevalent topics have higher mixture weight, and the weights sum to 1 for each article-in LDA, these can be interpreted as samples from a k-dimensional Dirichlet distribution. For the 1998-2020 period, we test the number of topics from k = 5 to 20. We consistently found that using approximately six topics produced the highest coherence score, and we confirmed these topics with subject matter expertise, in accordance with Hoyle et al. (2021) to address any eventual validation gap problem. The topics identified for 1998-2020 have high predictive validity (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013) in that they reflect the realities of protests in Hong Kong at the time in which the protests happened. This, in turn, serves as robustness tests for the high coherence score revealed by topic modeling.

paragraphs: Diachronic shifts in the understanding of a word are reflected in changes in the word's usage. Corpus linguistics recognizes this in shifts in collocational preferences: the word is used in a new pattern of sentential context as its meaning evolves. A methodological successor of this is the use of a smoothed notion of collocation based on word embeddings. There, the semantic shift manifests as change in the word's neighbors in an embedding space (Hamilton et al., 2016;Gonen et al., 2020). The same procedure can identify differences between words' usage when separated by something other than time. A word embedding model seeks to assign similar vectors (measured by dot product) to words in similar contexts, and different vectors to words in different contexts. If the usage of a word changes, then this should be reflected in changes to the word's context and consequent changes in the word's embedding. We both replicate and extend the difference-in-usage model of Gonen et al. (2020), which measures how the contexts of words differ. After this process, if w is used differently based on the presence or absence of the attribute, we expect its score to be quite small. Words whose usage does not depend on the attribute will have similar neighborhoods in each split. To extend the work of Gonen et al. (2020), we contextualize the similarity score of a given word against a reference set. We give the percentile in which word w's similarity score falls. We find this distributional measure to be more meaningful than the raw similarity score.

paragraphs: We apply computational sentiment analysis to measures the tone and connotations of articles. While it is common to use hand-crafted sentiment (valency) lexica (Mohammad, 2018), they are necessarily incomplete as sentiment depend on more than lexicon choice. We, therefore, chose a BERT-based model to classify a given sentence as positive or negative because of its near state-of-the-art sentiment classification abilities. We treat sentiment as a binary attribute (+, -) and use a probabilistic classifier trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SST-2; Socher et al., 2013). The logistic regression model uses Distil-BERT (Sanh et al., 2019) for feature extraction from text; DistilBERT has previously been used for sentiment analysis of product reviews (Büyüköz et al., 2020). We split each article into sentences, then classify each sentence. An article's sentiment is taken as the average sentiment over all of its sentences. Sentiment score may provide evidence of stylometric differences between newspapers sources.

sections.paper_id: Dore - 2023 - A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Newspapers Coverage of Hong Kong Protests Between 1998 and
sections.header_raw: Volume and Timing of News Coverage
sections.header_norm: volume and timing of news coverage
paragraphs: Protests are an important means in contemporary societies for citizens to voice their opinions and concerns and exercise their right to free speech. However, the protests' ability to communicate their messages and achieve their desired outcomes depends on whether and how the press, and the media more generally, portrays them (Agnone, 2007;King, 2011). As such, the volume and timing of articles about protests in Hong Kong make it possible to gauge the news value of protests as well as the thematic relevance to newspapers in general. 9Figure 1 shows that Hong Kong protests are newsworthy for the SCMP, the China Daily-and western-based newspapers, and that with the exception of 2014, the two Hong Kong-based newspapers publish more, and more consistently than their western-based counterparts. With 3347 articles, the SCMP published almost 8.8 times more articles than the China Daily (i.e., 3347 vs. 381), and more than any other newspapers in the Hong Kong Protest News Dataset. Moreover, the SCMP and China Daily together published 4.7 times more articles than the NYT, WSJ, WaPo, FT, the Guardian, and the Times of London combined (i.e., 3728 vs. 793). 10 In general, western-based newspapers' coverage declines over time, is punctuated by sharp peaks and dips, and is dominated by the NYT, which with 425 articles, published more than half of what all western-based newspapers published about Hong Kong protests. The 2014 Occupy Central protests emerge as an exception, with western-based newspapers publishing 55% more articles than the SCMP and the China Daily (i.e., 236 vs. 131). As the protests continued for 79 days, the SCMP and China Daily 11 carne gie-mec. org/ 2016/ 10/ 17/ impli catio ns-of-sixth-hong-kong-legis lative-elect ion-for-relat ions-withbeiji ng-pub-64872

coverage declined progressively perhaps in effort to avoid upsetting Beijing when chronicling a protest movement that quickly became China's most challenging political struggle since the Beijing June 4, 1989 protests.

As the main English-language printed newspapers in Hong Kong, it is not surprising that the SCMP and China Daily's coverage of Hong Kong protests is more frequent and voluminous than that of western-based newspapers, particularly nowadays as the foreign press has reduced the space, resources and commitment devoted to the topics it covers and, especially, cut back on-the-ground reporting of foreign news. However, even when these realities are factored in, the difference in coverage that our investigation has revealed remains significant. Moreover, the sustained voluminous coverage of the protests enabled the SCMP and the China Daily to set the agenda on the salience of the protests, and thereby influence Hongkongers' opinions on the protests. 11 For instance, 2016 was a year punctuated by multiple protests, including those against a new election procedure requiring candidates running for the LegCo elections to sign a form "acknowledging Hong Kong as an inseparable part of China."

sections.paper_id: Dore - 2023 - A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Newspapers Coverage of Hong Kong Protests Between 1998 and
paragraphs: How news is framed is one of the most powerful newspapers' rhetorical tools. Entman (1993) explains that "framing essentially involves selection and salience. To frame is to select some aspects of a perceived reality and make them more salient in a communicating text, in such a way as to promote a particular problem definition, causal interpretation, moral evaluation, and/or treatment recommendation for the item described" (1993: 52). As what is included is as important as what is excluded, the frames chosen to organize news stories can influence public attitudes toward the protests (Entman, 2007;McCluskey et al., 2009;McCombs, 2002;and Chong and Druckman, 2007). Following Boydstun et al. ( 2013 2022), we use topic models to find the frames used in the articles and use qualitative content analysis to validate them. In its ability to characterize articles by the topics they contain, automatically identifying the topics from corpora, topic modeling helps understand the relative relevance that a wide variety of factors may play in framing the news on Hong Kong protests. (Gans, 1979;Shoemaker & Reese, 1996). As such, what emerges from the topic modeling analysis should be understood as frames-used as intended by Resnik-,foot_6 unique and specific to the coverage of the SCMP and China Daily of the protests in Hong Kong between 1998 and 2020.

Source: Authors-adapted from the Policy Frames Codebook in Boydstun et al., (2013) Boydstun et al.'s Policy Frames HK-and westernbased newspapers frames Economic frames Finance Capacity and resources frames -Morality frames Students Fairness and equality frames -Constitutionality and jurisprudence frames LegCo Policy prescription and evaluation LegCo Law and order, crime and justice frames Police violence Security and defense frames -Health and safety frames -Quality of life frames -Cultural identity frames -Public opinion frames -Political frames Chief Executive External regulation and reputation frames Mainland Other frames -

As showed in Fig. 2, our unsupervised topic modeling reveals that between 1998 and 2020, the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers used only six frames to portray the protests in Hong Kong: Chief Executive, Finance, Mainland, Police violence, LegCo, and Activism and Democracy. In addition to the high coherence score, these topics have high predictive validity (Quinn et al. 2010) that is "measuring how well variation in topic usage correspondents with expected events" (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013: 21). We tested the generalizability of our findings on newspapers' framing choices against the Policy Frames Codebook (Boydstun et al. 2013), which "provides a general system for categorizing frames across policy issues (…) that are intended to apply to any policy issue and in any communication context". As shown in Table 2, we found consistency across 8 of the of the Policy Frames Codebook's 14 categories.

The fact that both sets of newspapers use a limited number of frames is important in several respects. First, it is counterintuitive considering how protests in Hong Kong have changed over time. While protests have long been an undercurrent in Hong Kong's political culture, large-scale protests became more frequent, provocative, aggressive, and confrontational over time. 13 Second, it is rhetorically skillful. The recurrency of a limited set of frames, known as thematic framing, increases the effectiveness of the protest paradigm, a pattern of coverage that, while varying in degree, focuses on the deviant, threatening, and violent aspects of protests (Weaver and Scacco, 2013;Boyle et al., 2012). When applying the protest paradigm, "newspapers tend to borrow the script of crime news, highlights the protesters' (strange) appearance and/or ignorance, portrays protests as ineffective, focuses on the theatrical aspects of the protests" (McLeod and Hertog, 1999: 190-194). For instance, qualitative content analysis shows that the Finance frame focuses on the negative consequences that the protests have on Hong Kong as an international financial hub and China's financial gateway and firewall. The Mainland frame highlights how the protests undermine Beijing's efforts to uphold the principles of one country, two systems" and would destroy the city's prosperity and stability." 14 The Chief Executive frame emphasizes how the protests undermine the government's duty to ensure that the rule of law prevails in Hong Kong: "this series of extremely violent acts is pushing Hong Kong to a very dangerous situation." 15 The Police Violence frame stresses the police's duty to maintain order in the face of the growing frequency and violence of the protests: "months of street violence is already undermining both local and foreign-run businesses. The economy is taking a hit. Hong Kong has always been about being safe and rich"; 16 "the impact of these events, which began with peaceful mass protests, has been dramatic. Hong Kong's long-held reputation for the maintenance of law and order has been shattered. Its status as one of the world's safest cities has, at least temporarily, been lost." 17 Content analysis also revealed that while the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers use the same frames and leverage the protest paradigm, they do so to support contrasting agendas. In fact, the emphasis of the western-based newspapers' coverage is on citizens' struggle for democracy, civil and political liberties, and autonomy. The protest paradigm is used to support their claim that radical and violent tactics are the only ones that would garner international reporting on the struggle for democracy in Hong Kong. In fact, in their characterization of the protests "as a pro-democracy revolution, as a fight for freedom and pluralism, the use of radical tactics and violence was seen as the most effective means for protesters to be heard." (McCluskey et al., 2009: 361). Such diverging interpretations of the same frames inevitably impacts or alters the public's perception of the protests taking place in Hong Kong and in doing so further our understanding of how cognitive dissonance across media outlets could be socially and politically consequential (Tsfati and Walter, 2019).

The treatment of the frame Force and Order in the context of the 2019 anti-ELAB protests helps illustrate these differences as well this frame' predictive validity (i.e., expected correspondence between a measure and exogenous events uninvolved in the measurement process). Figure 2 shows that in 2019-2020, the trends for the use of the Force and Order frame in Hong Kong-and western-based newspapers mimic each other, which points to the significance of this frame in the portrayal of the protests for both news sources. Between January 2019 and June 2020, coverage remains consistent across both news sources, even if the extent and peaks of coverage are significantly higher in Hong Kong-than westernbased newspapers. This is not surprising. According to the Minister for Security,foot_11 there were more than 1,200 public order events (e.g., marches, demonstrations, or protests) from June to December 2019.

Moreover, while the protests were initially peaceful, over time they deteriorated into violent clashes between protesters and the Police, creating a cycle of violence that the Hong Kong government was not able to break. The Police used traditional riot control tactics, and on several occasions failed to distinguish between the majority of protesters who were peaceful, and the minority who turned to violence. Public records, in fact, show that the police fired 16,000 canisters of tear gas, 10,000 rubber bullets, 2,000 bean bag rounds, and "arrested 7,700 protesters, 40% of whom were students" (Ng, 2020: 483). Perhaps predictably, the use of "indiscriminate" (Purbrick, 2019) force by the Police against both violent and peaceful protesters to restore order emerges as a prominent news frame. Western-based newspapers, however, used the Force and Order frame to highlight the unprecedented level of the violence that characterized the police response to the protests, and how the police's violence could be a telling tale of Beijing's meddling to come in Hong Kong's affairs. Hong Kong-based newspapers used the Force and Order frame to offer detailed, chronicled descriptions of both the police's and protesters' actions, render the forceful responses as "an appropriate level of force to restore order and bring offenders to justice" (Ibrahim and Lam, 2020: 291), give space to the police statements and comments describing the Hong Kong violence as home grown terrorism, and promote a narrative aiming to restore a positive and trusting image of "Asia's finest" (Sinclair, 1997;Ibrahim and Lam, 2020: 289).

The findings of our research are unique to Hong Kong and also consistent with earlier findings on the effects of repeated exposure to the same news frames, particularly when attempting to get ahead of other news outlets or setting an agenda (Fig. 3). The use and relevance of repetitive news framing increases when the news stakes are high and valueladen, or contentious issues are reported-as is the case for protests in volatile political environments (Schulz et al., 2014). Repetitive thematic framing consistently changes the weight that is attached to certain news considerations over others, and is often followed by repetitive information-search which makes news pluralism less appealing and persuasive (Price et al., 1997;Shen, 2004;Valkenburg et al., 1999). By combining thematic framing with the protest paradigm, the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers make readers more likely to remember the arguments presented within a specific news frame. This heightens the chance of them agreeing with a frame's advocated standpoint, especially when repetitions are spaced over longer periods of time (Fernandes, 2013;Christian and Lecheler, 2012;Stephens & Rains, 2011). Further, the employment of these rhetorical techniques over an extended period of time results in decreasing news pluralism related to how protests and corollary events are portrayed.

As showed in Fig. 2, our unsupervised topic modeling reveals that between 1998 and 2020, the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers used only six frames to portray the protests in Hong Kong: Chief Executive, Finance, Mainland, Police violence, LegCo, and Activism and Democracy. In addition to the high coherence score, these topics have high predictive validity (Quinn et al. 2010) that is "measuring how well variation in topic usage correspondents with expected events" (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013: 21). We tested the generalizability of our findings on newspapers' framing choices against the Policy Frames Codebook (Boydstun et al. 2013), which "provides a general system for categorizing frames across policy issues (…) that are intended to apply to any policy issue and in any communication context". As shown in Table 2, we found consistency across 8 of the of the Policy Frames Codebook's 14 categories.

The fact that both sets of newspapers use a limited number of frames is important in several respects. First, it is counterintuitive considering how protests in Hong Kong have changed over time. While protests have long been an undercurrent in Hong Kong's political culture, large-scale protests became more frequent, provocative, aggressive, and confrontational over time. 13 Second, it is rhetorically skillful. The recurrency of a limited set of frames, known as thematic framing, increases the effectiveness of the protest paradigm, a pattern of coverage that, while varying in degree, focuses on the deviant, threatening, and violent aspects of protests (Weaver and Scacco, 2013;Boyle et al., 2012). When applying the protest paradigm, "newspapers tend to borrow the script of crime news, highlights the protesters' (strange) appearance and/or ignorance, portrays protests as ineffective, focuses on the theatrical aspects of the protests" (McLeod and Hertog, 1999: 190-194). For instance, qualitative content analysis shows that the Finance frame focuses on the negative consequences that the protests have on Hong Kong as an international financial hub and China's financial gateway and firewall. The Mainland frame highlights how the protests undermine Beijing's efforts to uphold the principles of one country, two systems" and would destroy the city's prosperity and stability." 14 The Chief Executive frame emphasizes how the protests undermine the government's duty to ensure that the rule of law prevails in Hong Kong: "this series of extremely violent acts is pushing Hong Kong to a very dangerous situation." 15 The Police Violence frame stresses the police's duty to maintain order in the face of the growing frequency and violence of the protests: "months of street violence is already undermining both local and foreign-run businesses. The economy is taking a hit. Hong Kong has always been about being safe and rich"; 16 "the impact of these events, which began with peaceful mass protests, has been dramatic. Hong Kong's long-held reputation for the maintenance of law and order has been shattered. Its status as one of the world's safest cities has, at least temporarily, been lost." 17 Content analysis also revealed that while the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers use the same frames and leverage the protest paradigm, they do so to support contrasting agendas. In fact, the emphasis of the western-based newspapers' coverage is on citizens' struggle for democracy, civil and political liberties, and autonomy. The protest paradigm is used to support their claim that radical and violent tactics are the only ones that would garner international reporting on the struggle for democracy in Hong Kong. In fact, in their characterization of the protests "as a pro-democracy revolution, as a fight for freedom and pluralism, the use of radical tactics and violence was seen as the most effective means for protesters to be heard." (McCluskey et al., 2009: 361). Such diverging interpretations of the same frames inevitably impacts or alters the public's perception of the protests taking place in Hong Kong and in doing so further our understanding of how cognitive dissonance across media outlets could be socially and politically consequential (Tsfati and Walter, 2019).

The treatment of the frame Force and Order in the context of the 2019 anti-ELAB protests helps illustrate these differences as well this frame' predictive validity (i.e., expected correspondence between a measure and exogenous events uninvolved in the measurement process). Figure 2 shows that in 2019-2020, the trends for the use of the Force and Order frame in Hong Kong-and western-based newspapers mimic each other, which points to the significance of this frame in the portrayal of the protests for both news sources. Between January 2019 and June 2020, coverage remains consistent across both news sources, even if the extent and peaks of coverage are significantly higher in Hong Kong-than westernbased newspapers. This is not surprising. According to the Minister for Security,foot_11 there were more than 1,200 public order events (e.g., marches, demonstrations, or protests) from June to December 2019.

Moreover, while the protests were initially peaceful, over time they deteriorated into violent clashes between protesters and the Police, creating a cycle of violence that the Hong Kong government was not able to break. The Police used traditional riot control tactics, and on several occasions failed to distinguish between the majority of protesters who were peaceful, and the minority who turned to violence. Public records, in fact, show that the police fired 16,000 canisters of tear gas, 10,000 rubber bullets, 2,000 bean bag rounds, and "arrested 7,700 protesters, 40% of whom were students" (Ng, 2020: 483). Perhaps predictably, the use of "indiscriminate" (Purbrick, 2019) force by the Police against both violent and peaceful protesters to restore order emerges as a prominent news frame. Western-based newspapers, however, used the Force and Order frame to highlight the unprecedented level of the violence that characterized the police response to the protests, and how the police's violence could be a telling tale of Beijing's meddling to come in Hong Kong's affairs. Hong Kong-based newspapers used the Force and Order frame to offer detailed, chronicled descriptions of both the police's and protesters' actions, render the forceful responses as "an appropriate level of force to restore order and bring offenders to justice" (Ibrahim and Lam, 2020: 291), give space to the police statements and comments describing the Hong Kong violence as home grown terrorism, and promote a narrative aiming to restore a positive and trusting image of "Asia's finest" (Sinclair, 1997;Ibrahim and Lam, 2020: 289).

The findings of our research are unique to Hong Kong and also consistent with earlier findings on the effects of repeated exposure to the same news frames, particularly when attempting to get ahead of other news outlets or setting an agenda (Fig. 3). The use and relevance of repetitive news framing increases when the news stakes are high and valueladen, or contentious issues are reported-as is the case for protests in volatile political environments (Schulz et al., 2014). Repetitive thematic framing consistently changes the weight that is attached to certain news considerations over others, and is often followed by repetitive information-search which makes news pluralism less appealing and persuasive (Price et al., 1997;Shen, 2004;Valkenburg et al., 1999). By combining thematic framing with the protest paradigm, the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers make readers more likely to remember the arguments presented within a specific news frame. This heightens the chance of them agreeing with a frame's advocated standpoint, especially when repetitions are spaced over longer periods of time (Fernandes, 2013;Christian and Lecheler, 2012;Stephens & Rains, 2011). Further, the employment of these rhetorical techniques over an extended period of time results in decreasing news pluralism related to how protests and corollary events are portrayed.

How news is framed is one of the most powerful newspapers' rhetorical tools. Entman (1993) explains that "framing essentially involves selection and salience. To frame is to select some aspects of a perceived reality and make them more salient in a communicating text, in such a way as to promote a particular problem definition, causal interpretation, moral evaluation, and/or treatment recommendation for the item described" (1993: 52). As what is included is as important as what is excluded, the frames chosen to organize news stories can influence public attitudes toward the protests (Entman, 2007;McCluskey et al., 2009;McCombs, 2002;and Chong and Druckman, 2007). In its ability to characterize articles by the topics they contain, automatically identifying the topics from corpora, topic modeling helps understand the relative relevance that a wide variety of factors may play in framing the news on Hong Kong protests. sections.header_raw: Strategic Ambiguity in Newspapers' Use of Protest-Related Keywords
sections.header_norm: strategic ambiguity in newspapers use of protest related keywords
paragraphs: Having categorized salient frames, we now turn to how the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers use protest-relevant keywords 19 over time, and whether their use suggests shifts in journalistic practices. We first test for whether there are important differences in the frequencies of 19 expert selected, protest-related keywords: confront, confrontation, crackdown, democracy, freedom, freedom of speech, independence, occupation, protest, protests, resistance, rights, riot, rule of law, severe, tension, terrorism, terrorist, unrest. For one keyword at a time, we perform the following steps: (i) split the corpus in two by some categorical attribute; (ii) obtain the keyword's frequency in each article of both corpora; and then (iii) apply a statistical test to establish whether our categorical 19

For the purpose of this research, the term keyword is used in the information retrieval rather than the corpus linguistic sense, meaning a term that is statistically characteristic in a text. See also Douglas Biber and Randi Reppen (Eds.). ( 2015). The Cambridge Handbook of English Corpus Linguistics. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, pp. 90-105.

variable-in our case the location of the article's publisher-is associated with a variation in frequency. As our corpus shows a non-parametric distribution, we apply Mann-Whitney U, splitting by newspaper source and using the Holm-Bonferroni correction with significance level of α = 0.01, to understand whether any of the 19 keywords of interest has statistically significant differences in usage. The Mann-Whitney U, splitting by newspaper source and using the Holm-Bonferroni correction, shows that every word has statistically significant differences in usage Except severe. The same test, splitting by before and after June 2019, shows statistically significant differences only for five (out of the 19) keywords: protests, unrest, rights, rule of law, and democracy. For the Friedman's test with four categories-that is "west before June"; "west after June"; "Hong Kong before June"; and "Hong Kong after June"-no keyword showed statistically significant differences.
Our investigation shows that except for 2019-2020, the SCMP and the China Daily use the terms democracy and freedom less than all western-based newspapers combined.

Between 1998 and 2020, freedom appears 70 times and democracy 107 in the SCMP and the China Daily and 492 and 242 in western-based newspapers. In 2014, the SCMP and China Daily combined use freedom 18 times and democracy 34 times, whereas these terms appear 127 and 416 times respectively in western-based newspapers. These trends are reversed in 2019-2020, when the SCMP and China Daily use the terms freedom (755 vs. 365) and democracy (668 vs. 217) more than western-based newspapers (Figs. 3 and 4). The SCMP and the China Daily use the terms democracy and freedom as qualifiers more often than as nouns, and more often than western-based newspapers. The difference in frequencies may be rooted in the type of articles that the newspapers publish. Western-based newspapers tend to cast citizens' civic assertiveness as their fight for democracy and the freedoms that come with it, or resistance against authoritarian tightening that Hong Kong has been experiencing following the 1997 handover. This narrative may require a more frequent use/discussion of democracy and freedom as concepts and values. The SCMP and the China Daily, on the other hand, focus their discourse narrowly on the details of the protests rather than on their meaning or reasons behind them. Such specific narrative, the terms democracy and freedom are used sparingly, and as qualifiers across the large number of articles.

We also see these findings as telling of how the SCMP and China Daily respond to Beijing's "strategic ambiguity" (Cheung, 2003: 210-225), which obfuscates what is and is not allowed journalistically, while at the same time creating more opportunities for selfcensorship (Lee, 2014). Hong Kong journalists and newspapers have established "strategic rituals" (Lee, 2000: 317) to limit and manage the impact of strategic ambiguity. These the use of polls as objective indicators of public opinions, emphasis on hard facts, and the repetition of formal titles of news sources. Strategic rituals can constrain diversity of opinions and be seen as self-censorship in disguise rather than a shield against political influence (Glasser, 1992).

sections.header_raw: Embedding Neighborhoods: How are Terms Used Differently Between the West and Hong Kong?
sections.header_norm: embedding neighborhoods how are terms used differently between the west and hong kong
paragraphs: The investigation of word embedding neighborhoods furthers our understanding of how words are used differently among the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers, and how the contexts of protest-related keywords differ across news sources. Building on Gonen et al. (2020), the analysis of lexical usage reveals semantic divergence in certain keywords between the two sets of news sources between 1998 and 2020, and also with regards to particular episodes of protests. As with the analysis of lexical frequencies, these rhetorical choices signal subtle but sustained rhetorical shifts, which reshape journalistic practices over time.

Table 3 shows that, between 1998 and 2020, the least semantic divergence is found in the lexical usage of the words riot (98th percentile), protest (88th percentile), occupation (80th percentile), whose respective nearest neighbors are consistently adversarial words. In the case of riot, nearest neighbors in Hong Kong-based newspapers include fired, barricade, and teargas. In western-based newspapers, we found mob, rampage, clash and radical. We see these trends as further evidence of the two sets of newspapers leveraging the protest paradigm for different reasons. The SCMP and the China Daily do so "to question the legitimacy of the protests or the protest groups, to marginalize the protests accentuating the deviance of the protesters from the mainstream public" (McLeod and Hertog, 1999: 311), whereas western-based newspapers to strengthen the legitimacy of the protests and/ or the protesters. In the case of unrest, democracy, rights, crackdown, and protest, our analysis found significant differences in the way these terms were used in newspapers before and after July 1, 2019. We also sought to quantify the degree to which the introduction of ELAB acted as a pivotal moment in how the newspapers in our dataset portray those protests, and found that June 2019 emerges as a turning point, after which the meaning of several keywords shifts for at least the remainder of 2019. When we split the corpus into "pre-June 30th, 2019" and "post-June 30th, 2019", neighborhood shift analysis reveals significant high scores for resistance, severe, riots, confront, confrontation, and terrorism. This suggests that the context and/or semantics of these words changes through 2019, regardless of whether Hong Kong-or western-based news sources are considered. For instance, in the first half of 2019, neighbors for riots include actions, open, engage, and taken, which are not charged and in the context of either a reporting or an opinion piece descriptively inform readers. However, in the second half of 2019, the neighboring terms for riots change to include more polarizing terms such as violent, escalated, destructive, triggered, anti-government, and sparked. These findings reflect well the extent to which June 2019 was a pivotal moment during the 2019-2020 protests, and that as the protests escalated, citizens' civic assertiveness was described more harshly and with a wealth of details to highlight the negative impacts.

sections.paper_id: Dore - 2023 - A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Newspapers Coverage of Hong Kong Protests Between 1998 and
paragraphs: Computational sentiment analysis revealed that, between 1998 and 2020, the articles' average sentiment of the SCMP and the China Daily and that of western-based articles is broadly the same (36.9% vs. 38.1%). Our analysis, however, shows that there is wide variation across news sources.

At 40%, the China Daily has the most positive tone among all newspapers in our dataset, whereas at 31.3% and 31.5% the SCMP and The Times emerge as the newspapers with the most negative tone. The Guardian and The Financial Times (at 32.9% and 33.9% respectively) also rank low in positivity, which makes the UK-based newspapers the more negative about Hong Kong protests among western-based newspapers. We see this finding as the legacy of the UK's mixed feelings regarding Hong Kong's handover to China, in spite of Prime Minister Thatcher's 1984 statement that "We have got an agreement which is acceptable overwhelmingly to the people of Hong Kong. That agreement will extend into 50 years beyond 1997. I feel we have done a good job for the people of Hong Kong."foot_12 Finally, US-based newspapers average a positivity score of 36%, with the NYT articles being almost imperceptibly more positive than those of the WSJ (i.e., 36.3% vs. 36.1%) and WaPo (i.e., 36.3% vs. 36.0%).

sections.paper_id: Dore - 2023 - A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Newspapers Coverage of Hong Kong Protests Between 1998 and
paragraphs: The investigation of how Hong Kong-and western-based newspapers cover protests in Hong Kong broadened the application of NLP techniques to the social science (Atteveldt and Peng, 2018;Hilbert et al., 2019;Baden et al. 2021;and Brady, 2019) and the findings from the research that informs this article contribute to fill gaps in the literature on Hong Kong media, particularly changes in journalistic practices between 1998 and 2020 (Tsfati and Walter 2019).

The application of NLP techniques and a contextualized understanding of the volume and timing of articles published, revealed that between 1998 and 2020, the SCMP and the China Daily published more articles about protests in Hong Kong than western-based newspapers except for 2014 when this trend was reversed. The coverage of the two Hong Kong-based newspapers is consistent and sustained over time, whereas that of westernbased newspapers is punctuated by sharps peaks and dips, and declines, most significantly between 2015 and 2020. Such marked differences created an opportunity for the SCMP and the China Daily to set the agenda for how protests are framed and reported. Over time, these practices have resulted in a less critical press, especially towards Hong Kong's issues that are of relevance to Beijing (Cheung 2003;Lee 2007). Moreover, the way in which SCMP and the China Daily frame their coverage of protests reveals the use of thematic framing which increases the effectiveness of the protest paradigm in voicing criticism towards the protests (Lee 2014). Editorially, thematic framing makes it easier for newspapers to have people identify the issue at the heart of the protests as a society-wide phenomenon rather than citizens' discontent directed at specific actions of the Hong Kong Administration or Beijing's government (Aarøe & Petersen, 2018;Hart 2010).

Stylometric differences (Bergsma et al. 2012) across news sources reveal that the SCMP is the newspaper with the most negative tone in its coverage of the protests among the newspapers in our database. Also, the China Daily and SCMP use the terms democracy and freedom less frequently than western-based newspapers and, generally, as qualifiers rather than as nouns. These rhetorical choices may be attributable to the type of articles that the SCMP and the China Daily publish when covering the protests. Our investigation of word embedding neighborhoods revealed that protest-related keywords are used differently between the SCMP, the China Daily and western-based newspapers and that the contexts of protest-related keywords differ across news sources over time. Our research confirmed semantic divergence in certain keywords between Hong Kong-and westernbased newspapers vis-à-vis particular episodes of protests (Hamilton et al., 2016). We also uncovered differences-in-differences in how the SCMP and the China Daily report on protests over time. In the case of the 2019-2020 anti-ELAB protests, the semantic context of the protest keywords in these two newspapers became more polarizing, compared with that of western-based newspapers. June 2019 emerges as a pivotal moment, after which the meaning of several keywords shifts for at least the remainder of 2019. In the first half of 2019, in fact, neighbors for riots include terms like actions, open, engage, and taken which are not charged and, in the context of either a reporting or an opinion piece, descriptively inform readers. However, in the second half of 2019, the nature of the neighboring terms for riots changes to include more polarizing terms such as violent, escalated, destructive, and sparked.

The rhetorical and non-rhetorical tactics uncovered by the NLP analysis provide empirical evidence of changes in patterns of journalistic practice following the 1997 handover (Chan et al., 1996;Lee and Li 2006;Lau and To 2020;Lee and Chan 2018). This, in turn, helps filling gaps in the literature on Hong Kong printed media (Chan & Lee, 1991;McQuail and Windahl 1993;Lee and Chu 1998), in the context of ongoing work on media effect (Oliver et al. 2019) strategic interaction and cultural co-orientation (Fung and Lee 1994;Yeung 2000;Lee 2007). While the use of these tactics made covering issues involving conflict between Mainland China and Hong Kong less problematic (Lee, 2000) for the Hong Kong print media, it eliminated neither the challenge of covering hot issues in ways acceptable to Beijing nor the risk of becoming hostage to pressure from powerful local public opinion (Ngok, 2007). Moreover, these changes are making it increasingly difficult for the western-based press to balance being an effective watchdog with managing its relationship with a politically assertive China (Dore, 2022).
